name: Rosa Mansilla
bio: For three generations her family has raised sheep on the wide grasslands of Río Verde, three hours north of Punta Arenas. Rosa shears, washes, cards and spins the wool herself — dyeing it with calafate berries, lichen, and chañar bark gathered from the steppe.
quote: The wool here is different. The wind makes it strong. You can feel where it comes from.

name: Héctor Vargas
bio: Héctor learned woodworking from his father in a workshop that still uses hand planes older than either of them. He works exclusively with native lenga beech — salvaged timber from wind-felled trees on his family's land south of the city.
quote: Lenga is not furniture wood. It's the forest distilled. You have to earn the right to cut it.

name: Carmen Díaz
bio: Carmen spent twenty years as a cook in estancia kitchens before founding Conservas Magallanes in her home kitchen. Her small-batch preserves use fruit and spices that grow only in this latitude — calafate, rhubarb, merkén pepper.
quote: Everything I make, I make in quantities I can count. That's the only way to keep it honest.

name: Andrés Kusanovic
bio: Third-generation saddler. His grandfather made gear for the big sheep estancias; Andrés pivoted toward objects for people who travel — mate holders, belts, card wallets — using the same estancia-tanned leather, the same waxed thread.
quote: A saddle and a card wallet are the same problem: the leather has to last longer than the person.

name: Miriam Yáñez
bio: Miriam trained as a ceramicist in Valparaíso and returned south to work with Patagonian mineral glazes — copper, iron oxide, manganese drawn from the steppe and the coast. Her palette is the palette of the landscape.
quote: I'm not trying to make pretty things. I'm trying to make things that remember where they came from.
